items.title: Sanctions screening
items.description: Sanctions screening compares customers, counterparties, and transaction parties against applicable sanctions lists to identify potential matches that require attention. It is typically run at onboarding, on list updates, and at the point of payment. A potential match normally halts processing pending review rather than being cleared automatically. In software, screening should be auditable, capturing what was screened, against which list version, and the disposition of any hit.
items.applicability: Applies to onboarding and payment processing in banking and management-company middleware where parties must be screened.
items.example: An outgoing payment screens the beneficiary against the current sanctions list version; a potential hit holds the payment and routes it to a reviewer.
items.limitation: Awareness-level only and NOT legal, regulatory, or compliance advice. Sanctions obligations are jurisdiction-specific; a screening hit is a signal to review, not a legal determination. Confirm against current applicable lists, laws, and approved procedures.

items.title: PEP screening
items.description: Politically Exposed Person (PEP) screening identifies customers or connected parties who hold or held prominent public roles, or are close associates, because such relationships can carry elevated risk. A PEP indication usually triggers enhanced scrutiny and senior approval rather than automatic refusal. Screening considers the person's status and the strength of the match, and the outcome is reviewed and recorded. Systems should treat a PEP flag as a risk input that drives EDD, not as a verdict on the relationship.
items.applicability: Applies to onboarding and ongoing review in banking and management-company systems where elevated-risk relationships must be identified.
items.example: A new customer matches a PEP reference; the case is escalated for enhanced due diligence and senior sign-off, with the decision and rationale recorded.
items.limitation: Awareness-level only and NOT legal or compliance advice. PEP definitions and handling are jurisdiction-specific; a PEP flag is a risk signal, not a legal determination. Confirm against current laws and approved procedures.

items.title: Adverse media screening
items.description: Adverse media screening searches public information for negative news potentially relevant to a customer's risk, such as reported involvement in financial crime. Results are signals that must be assessed for relevance, recency, and reliability rather than treated as fact. The review records whether the media relates to the customer and how it affects the risk view. Software should capture the source and the analyst's assessment so the basis of any risk change is transparent.
items.applicability: Applies to onboarding and ongoing monitoring in banking and management-company environments using negative-news checks as a risk input.
items.example: A media hit suggesting past wrongdoing is reviewed; the analyst confirms it concerns the customer, judges it material, and raises the risk rating with the source recorded.
items.limitation: Awareness-level only and NOT legal or compliance advice. Adverse media is unverified signal, not proof; relevance and reliability must be assessed. A hit is not a legal determination. Confirm handling against jurisdiction-specific laws and approved procedures.

items.title: Transaction monitoring scenarios
items.description: Transaction monitoring applies rules or models, often called scenarios, to detect activity that is unusual relative to a customer's expected profile, such as sudden spikes, structuring-like patterns, or unexpected geographies. Scenarios generate alerts that are investigated and dispositioned, not automatic conclusions. Good design tunes thresholds to balance detection against false-positive volume and records why each alert was raised. Systems should keep scenario logic and parameters versioned so historical alerts remain explainable.
items.applicability: Applies to ongoing monitoring components in banking and management-company middleware that observe transaction behaviour over time.
items.example: A scenario flags a series of payments just under a reporting threshold; the alert is investigated and either escalated or closed with a recorded rationale.
items.limitation: Awareness-level only and NOT legal or compliance advice. An alert indicates possible unusual activity to review, not a legal determination of wrongdoing. Scenario design is jurisdiction- and institution-specific; confirm against current laws and approved procedures.

items.title: Suspicious-activity escalation (awareness-level)
items.description: When monitoring or review surfaces activity that appears unusual or potentially suspicious, institutions typically have an internal escalation path to a designated function for assessment and decision. At the software level, the relevant capability is reliable, confidential routing of a case to that function with the supporting evidence preserved. The decision on any external action rests entirely with qualified compliance staff following the institution's procedures and applicable law. Engineers should ensure the escalation is recorded and access-controlled, without the system itself drawing conclusions.
items.applicability: Applies when building case-management and escalation features that route potential concerns to a compliance function in banking middleware.
items.example: An investigator escalates an unresolved alert to the designated internal function with the evidence attached; the system records the handoff and restricts visibility appropriately.
items.limitation: Awareness-level only and NOT legal or compliance advice and NOT guidance on reporting obligations. Whether and how to report is a jurisdiction-specific legal matter decided by qualified staff. The system routes and records; it does not determine suspicion or legal duty. Confirm against current laws and approved procedures.

items.title: Transaction approval workflow
items.description: A transaction approval workflow governs how an instruction is authorised before execution, combining validation, screening checks, limit checks, and the required human approvals. Higher-risk or higher-value transactions typically demand additional approvals or screening clearance before release. Each decision point records its actor and outcome, and any failed check diverts the item to review rather than proceeding. The workflow should be deterministic so the exact path of any past transaction can be reconstructed for audit.
items.applicability: Applies to payment and instruction processing in banking and management-company middleware where approvals and checks gate execution.
items.example: A cross-border payment passes validation, clears sanctions screening, satisfies a within-limit check, receives the required approval, and is released; a failed screening check holds it instead.
items.limitation: Awareness-level only and NOT legal or compliance advice. Required checks and approval tiers are jurisdiction- and institution-specific; passing the workflow is not a legal determination. Confirm against current laws and approved procedures.

items.title: Payment approval limits
items.description: Payment approval limits define value thresholds that determine how much authority is required to release a payment, with larger amounts demanding more or more senior approvers. Limits may be set per user, per role, per account, or per product, and are enforced at the point of approval. Breaching a limit should block release and route to a higher authority rather than allowing an override without record. Systems should evaluate limits server-side and log both the applicable limit and the resulting decision.
items.applicability: Applies to payment authorisation controls in banking and management-company middleware.
items.example: A payment above a user's individual limit requires a second, more senior approver; the system records which limit applied and who provided the additional authority.
items.limitation: Awareness-level only and NOT legal or compliance advice. Limit structures are institution-specific and must be confirmed against approved authority policy. Not a legal determination.

items.title: Maker/checker for high-risk transactions
items.description: For high-risk transactions, dual-control maker-checker is commonly strengthened so that initiation and authorisation are performed by different, suitably authorised people, sometimes with an additional compliance check. The intent is to ensure elevated-risk activity receives independent scrutiny before release. The control records each role's actor and prevents the same identity from filling conflicting roles. In software, the risk classification of the transaction should drive how many and which approvals are mandatory.
items.applicability: Applies to authorisation of elevated-risk transactions in banking and management-company middleware.
items.example: A high-risk cross-border payment requires a maker, an independent checker, and a compliance approval before release, with each step attributed to a distinct user.
items.limitation: Awareness-level only and NOT legal or compliance advice. What counts as high-risk and the required controls are jurisdiction- and institution-specific; applying the control is not a legal determination. Confirm against current laws and approved procedures.
